tree.title: JJ — the first tree
tree.note: Grown from the concluded diary. jjconnect.jp is the seed; Rakusat has become its own tree.

nodes.title: jjconnect.jp
nodes.body: The company platform, online 2026-02-06. The seed everything else grew from.

nodes.title: 株式会社ＪＪ ＣＯＮＮＥＣＴ
nodes.body: 法人番号 3011001151860 · 設立 令和5年1月 · 資本金 700万 · 本店 新宿区北新宿. The legal ground it all stands on.

nodes.title: 朱麗娟 — 代表取締役
nodes.body: Founder & sole director. The hands that tend the tree.

nodes.title: Moat = dataset + judgment
nodes.body: The engine is swappable compute; what compounds is the data and the judgment on top.

nodes.title: Info-only line
nodes.body: Information & analysis only — no 媒介 / 代行 / 非弁. What lets a 法人 run without a 宅建業免許.

nodes.title: Build for exit
nodes.body: Portability is the real driver — repos, domains, the Supabase cutover all keep it sellable and handover-ready.

nodes.title: Ship early, but hot-fix is dangerous
nodes.body: Cut over as soon as it's safe; a push to main is a prod deploy — diagnose and reproduce first.

nodes.title: jjconnect.jp · 会社サイト
The build-out
nodes.body: A heavy month of platform engineering: the role matrix and RLS strategy, the TipTap publish flow, an admin console, a search page with migrations, third-party account integration.

nodes.title: The Auth0 login firefight
nodes.body: An extended battle just to keep login standing: Auth0 OIDC kept throwing 500s and login loops, and the fixes piled up around redirect_uri / www-origin handling, apex→www redirects, sharing the Supabase session with the navbar, even a temporary "bypass auth for testing" to break the loop.

nodes.title: Brand polish, then Auth0 restored
nodes.body: Front-page identity work: the tagline became つなぐ、その先へ。, the slogan "GET CONNECTED, GET BEYOND," favicon and logo restored, the Services pages translated.

nodes.title: The ".jp major review" — strip back to Supabase-only
nodes.body: The cleanup.

nodes.title: The modern rebuild
nodes.body: Rebuilt the site on a real design system — shadcn/ui on Tailwind, a React nav and footer with a JA/ZH/EN switcher, a next-intl i18n scaffold — and ported the brochure content to React routes.

nodes.title: The domains get suspended
nodes.body: I found it in a junk-mail folder: お名前 / GMO had flagged my account for 本人性確認 — registrant identity verification — and auto-suspended both jjconnect.online and jjconnect.jp when the deadline passed (NXDOMAIN, not a hijack, not billing).

nodes.title: 落札ラボ / Rakusat
The flyer gadget that started everything
nodes.body: It didn't begin as a platform.

nodes.title: Auth0, the original sin
nodes.body: I bolted Auth0 login onto the gadget to gate it, and that one early choice — staff and clients sharing one UI behind one auth layer — became the root of months of pain.

nodes.title: The three-day contract
nodes.body: I wrote DECISIONS.md as a binding execution contract and gave it one goal: a clean, reliable mobile field-staff UI — photo upload and on-site records — in three days.

nodes.title: The rules I set down
nodes.body: The same day I set the rules I'd hold to.

nodes.title: The field tool goes live
nodes.body: The field survey tool went to production at jjconnect.online/rakusat, with the launch checklist as the human gate — a real-phone Safari + Chrome run of the photo-upload → submit → persistence path.

nodes.title: What to share when clients are competing
nodes.body: I sat with a genuinely hard product question: my instinct is to share data as widely as possible, but my clients bid against each other on the same 物件.

nodes.title: The migration begins, on a branch
nodes.body: Kicked off the Supabase Postgres + Auth foundation strictly on a branch — production untouched, main stays TEST_MODE, zero migration code on main — to be flipped only at a controlled cutover.

nodes.title: The mobile "?" placeholders
nodes.body: Property photos showed broken-image placeholders on every non-owner phone — they were hotlinked straight from private Google Drive URLs that only render in a browser already logged into my Google account, so clients got Google's sign-in HTML instead of a picture.

nodes.title: The access model: agents as symmetric private tenants
nodes.body: Locked the model the whole platform rests on: Agent A / B / C are three distinct agent teams — symmetric private tenants on shared facts — each owning its own field / analysis / decisions via per-owner RLS, each publishing to its own clients by tier.

nodes.title: Stages 4–6 ready behind flags
nodes.body: In one intensive push, built and verified the Supabase data paths for the three big surfaces — field, analysis, properties — each behind a backend flag defaulting to sheets, so prod changed by exactly nothing.

nodes.title: Soft launch — live, but only auth went soft
nodes.body: Took the soft launch to prod via blue/green: built the deployment without flipping the domain, verified it on its own URL, then promoted with zero downtime.

nodes.title: The report button learns to find its PDF
nodes.body: The 三点セット button always 404'd because the server looked for a PDF named after 物件命名, but the real court files are named by case number — TYO_R07N00324_1_OCR.pdf — with kana→letter mapping (ヌ→N, ケ→K), zero-padding, and an _OCR tail nothing bridged.

nodes.title: Owning the invitation
nodes.body: Supabase's recovery links expire in about an hour — the wrong primitive for a seven-day window and clients who log in once a fortnight.

nodes.title: The landing flash, fixed right (+ color ships)
nodes.body: The hero re-derived "latest batch" on every page load by downloading and parsing every batch's master sheet — cold-start lag and a permanently-stale 5A flash.

nodes.title: The soft launch failed — so go the hard way
nodes.body: The clever fourteen-day soft-kickoff plan failed, and I suspected the soft mechanism itself was the cause.

nodes.title: Freeze the analysis sheet
nodes.body: I recognized that analysis fields churn far faster than facts — "current analysis report is just a beginning, and we will improve it a lot much [more] frequent than field report" — and that opinion data has two dimensions a flat sheet can't hold.

nodes.title: Cut over as soon as safe — humans forget
nodes.body: With field reports working, I pushed to ship the parked profile/watchlist work rather than let it sit on a branch.

nodes.title: The red flag I didn't want anyone to see
nodes.body: I owned a data mistake: I'd copied the 5B master sheet without clearing its data, a stale URL rode into the database, and a wrong 警示 (red flag) surfaced on a property.

nodes.title: CLAUDE.md is the source of repeated regression
nodes.body: After the auth cutover went bad, I traced the deeper cause to my own CLAUDE.md: it held the original plan and had never been updated, so agents kept regressing against stale instructions.

nodes.title: The cutover incident — a push to main hit prod, and login looped
nodes.body: The hard cutover hit production instantly, because a push to main is a prod deploy — and it broke login into a repeat-login loop.

nodes.title: A "private layer" bypassed all my vetting — set a boundary
nodes.body: What scared me most in the incident wasn't the bug — it was that a 私密层 redirect had bypassed all the coding and testing skills I rely on, landing unvetted code on prod, and that I'd silently lost track of at least two user seats.

nodes.title: The auth rescue — front door rebuilt, every flag deleted
nodes.body: Post-incident, the systematization shipped in four verified passes (suite + browser-E2E green on a preview → promote).

nodes.title: Kill anything no longer needed — dead code misleads
nodes.body: Heading into the read-flip I set a cleanup rule: "we should kill anything no longer needed.

nodes.title: The auth matrix is mine to define
nodes.body: I took ownership of authorization policy by putting it in a Google Sheet — the auth matrix — that the system enforces.

nodes.title: The read-flip — the milestone, and "updates only when I say so"
nodes.body: I scheduled the Supabase read-flip for 5am as a deliberate, irreversible-feeling milestone: after it, all data lives in the database and the Sheet is merely a backup.

nodes.title: The verifier consolidation — the #1 drift liability, resolved
nodes.body: The auth incident's root class was that every endpoint carried its own copy of the JWT verifier, and the copies drifted.

nodes.title: The 例 wizard, revived clean onto main
nodes.body: The /rakusat/input/ 404 turned out not to be a read-flip regression — the 例 三点セット wizard had never been on prod, parked on the retired cutover branch, 404 by design.

nodes.title: ENFORCE_CAPS — the check that was stranded
nodes.body: I discovered the write-caps gate was stranded: it lived only inside the Supabase backend branch, but prod runs the default sheets backend, so saves fell through ungated — flipping the env var alone would have done nothing.

nodes.title: 200× — killing the 25-second batches scan
nodes.body: "Loading faster" was the whole brief, so I measured on prod first: every batches view cost 25–53 seconds on a Google Drive scan reused by ~9 callers, and my daily-active users bypassed the CDN cache that only ever helped guests.

nodes.title: The auth-matrix sync button (and a dead-invites bug it exposed)
nodes.body: Made the Sheet's auth matrix tab the single source of truth, applied to Supabase profiles by an owner-clicked 权限同步 button — preview-diff → confirm → apply, never auto-revoking.

nodes.title: The reading reckoning — and "the gap was mine, not DocAI's"
nodes.body: 6B uses full original court reports (which read better than OCR-layered copies), but DocAI's sync endpoint 500'd on most at 30 pages, so I capped it to 10.

nodes.title: The input UI becomes a data-maintenance platform
nodes.body: Grew the 例 wizard from a one-shot input form into the formal maintenance entrance for any batch, including closed and published ones.

nodes.title: The first real 6B staff test — and the bug that blocked submit
nodes.body: The first live 6B staff run (report 687, ブリシア西新井) shipped a batch of fixes each verified against 687 first: katakana-led building names that the Latin-only pattern had left empty, a bare 23-ward 区 now resolving to 东京都, and a 提交概要 that lists all fields with ⚠️ 空缺 flags ("空缺信息更重要").

nodes.title: The 物件命名 rule I misread, and had to revert
nodes.body: With submit finally reachable, I briefly misread your restatement of the naming rule as "drop the 区" and shipped that — then you corrected me, "旧规则正确," and I reverted.

nodes.title: delete_property, and the 429 that broke submit
nodes.body: A property had landed during the wrong-name window and there was no way to remove it — so I built action=delete_property and a 🗑 button to clean it up.

nodes.title: Accumulate reading skill — never replace
nodes.body: Watching the parser meet a structurally different report (令和7年(ヌ)第310号 — 強制競売, 共有持分, 木造戸建 on leased land) I set the rule for all parser work: "the report is mostly the same format, but different in some detail.

nodes.title: The parsing skill is an asset that grows as we use it
nodes.body: This is the idea I'd been circling for weeks, finally said plainly: "the rakusalab parsing skill is an independent asset — it grows as we use it." The code is disposable; what compounds is the spec-of-reality underneath it — fixtures, tests, glossary, rules, taxonomy — and that survives any engine swap.

nodes.title: Hot fix is dangerous
nodes.body: The hardest lesson, from the field-survey incident.

nodes.title: 警示 ⚠ and 关注 / 取下 — the signals on the card
nodes.body: Built two new public signals across data, AI, and UI in three staged commits, shipped the same day.

nodes.title: Old batches get their own master sheet, explicitly
nodes.body: Submit on the 2026-1 batch 400'd because its master sheet names the data tab plainly 入例表 while the code expected 入例表【土地+建物】.

nodes.title: The workflow: Gemini captures, the parser wins, the human finalizes
nodes.body: I settled the division of labor and asked to build it right away: "Gemini is a data capturer, parser is skill sets rendering better results, human finalizes every data." Shipped the 🧠通读 trial (one Gemini call over the full OCR, filling only empty fields, each marked purple with its exact ¥/$ cost) and the feedback loop behind it: every source's proposal and every human correction land in a read_feedback table — server-scored so a client bug can't corrupt the signal — and a 读取质量 admin tab aggregates per-source accuracy and points at the fields to sharpen.

nodes.title: One prompt always grows too long — so impose structure
nodes.body: A too-long extraction prompt caused a regression — a "fill 无 if unmentioned" rule bleeding across fields — and I refused the easy patch.

nodes.title: We don't invent technique — we build skills; tools are swappable
nodes.body: I separated the durable layer from the disposable one for good.

nodes.title: 先进入例 — the secret weapon
nodes.body: Committed to building 先进入例 — a hidden, owner-only, registry-driven 三点セット wizard to replace the 例 tool entirely, behind a deliberately hard-to-guess path inside the admin page, invisible even to other admins.

nodes.title: Strategy · 経営革新
Rakusat is a platform, not a field tool
nodes.body: I named the real ambition out loud: this isn't an internal field tool, it's the base of a platform.

nodes.title: Lock the foundation — Supabase, facts shared / judgment private
nodes.body: Committed the data-and-auth model for the real platform: Supabase (Postgres + Auth + RLS), media on Drive, three roles (client / agent / staff) with agent-teams and client-orgs paired one-to-one, and a "facts shared, judgment private" sharing model.

nodes.title: The why above the platform — be #1 in 競売
nodes.body: I set the north star above the product: build the most successful court-auction (競売) real-estate business in Japan, with no ceiling.

nodes.title: One function budget — give 道 its own project
nodes.body: Hitting Vercel's 12-function ceiling (I'd already consolidated 12 endpoints down to four), I decided 道 should deploy as its own Vercel project rather than crowd the auction tool's budget.

nodes.title: Topology as a client-profile and optionality play
nodes.body: I chose the multi-domain shape deliberately, and the reasoning is strategic, not technical: jjconnect.jp is the company / CMS face, jjconnect.online is the production center for the tools, with the Tokyo auction tools as the first branch and a second already scheduled.

nodes.title: Shared auth follows shared business, not shared owner
nodes.body: I refined the auth boundary into a principle I want to hold: shared auth follows shared business, not shared owner.

nodes.title: The parsing skill is an independent compounding asset
nodes.body: I reframed how I think about parser work: "the rakusalab parsing skill is an independent asset — it grows as we use it." A fix becomes an asset only when it deposits durable knowledge that lowers the cost of the next problem.

nodes.title: Commercialization — info-only, to dodge the 宅建業 license
nodes.body: I moved Rakusalab from build to commercialization and locked the legal posture the whole business hangs on: the service is information and analysis only — clients receive curated 競売 data and analysis and bid/buy on their own.

nodes.title: The launch gate is Track A; B and C don't block
nodes.body: Defined "Ground Zero" — the public commercial launch — gated only by Track A (commercial readiness): legal docs (rebuild the privacy policy, which currently falsely says "internal tool" and becomes a liability on launch, plus 特商法 / 利用規約 / 免責), a security perimeter (captcha, rate-limit, tested restore, breach-notification), and paid infra flipped launch-eve since nothing in the build phase needs it.

nodes.title: i18n is audience-tiered — public is Japanese-canonical
nodes.body: On i18n the leverage is scope-tiering, not a rebuild.

nodes.title: The real migration driver is handover and sale
nodes.body: When I mused about an "overall migration," the real driver — once I named it honestly — is handover and sale: assigning admin to someone else, or selling the whole repo + business.

nodes.title: The moat isn't extraction — it's the dataset + judgment
nodes.body: Sharpened the asset thesis: extraction (OCR/LLM) is commoditizing — a burst is coming, so ride Google rather than fight it.

nodes.title: The 経営革新計画 application begins — a B2B pivot
nodes.body: Started the Tokyo 経営革新計画 承認申請 (Management Innovation Plan; approval unlocks support measures).

nodes.title: Entity identity must match the 登記, exactly
nodes.body: Pulling the 履歴事項全部証明書 forced a precision call: the filing entity is 株式会社ＪＪ ＣＯＮＮＥＣＴ (registered full-width, with a space) — the business plan's "JJconnect" is informal, and the application must match the 登記.

nodes.title: Cost is settled — optimize for accuracy, not cheapness
nodes.body: I checked the reading pipeline's economics against my actual GCP bill and made the call: cost is not a constraint, so optimize for accuracy.

nodes.title: v3 of the application — financials are the last gap
nodes.body: The application reached v3 with all the company and registration data filled (v1/v2 superseded and trashed).

nodes.title: Segregate the crown jewel — hidden owner-only, decoy in plain sight
nodes.body: After discovering the reading playbook was publicly served — the "读取技能资产" sat in a web-served JSON, the core IP free for the taking — I sealed the leak (moved it to a server-only module, locked by a leak test) and made a strategic call about how the asset is protected.

nodes.title: 道 / Do
道 is the destination — but let it emerge
nodes.body: When Claude proposed the full expected-value / win-probability bidding engine, I said it was right in almost every word — and premature.

nodes.title: Design locked — three UIs and an AI black box at two touchpoints
nodes.body: Scoped stage 1 as three plain UIs — a focused-property worklist, a per-property decision canvas (considerations → answers → pinned evidence → recorded opinions → decision), and a people-and-tasks log — over private-by-default do_ tables with RLS, explicitly not Sheets.

nodes.title: 道 is the migration's first customer
nodes.body: I confirmed the whole 道 build is sequenced after the Supabase DB + auth migration, and the reason is structural: 道 is private judgment — verdicts, bids, opinions — and "share facts, keep judgment private" requires the app to enforce who sees what, which is Row-Level Security, which can't exist on Sheets while the app is still TEST_MODE.

nodes.title: Record my exact thinking, separately
nodes.body: I asked for a verbatim record of the whole 道 design conversation — "every words both of you and me" — kept apart from the other files, "I need to remember my thinking in detail." This is the same impulse that produced this diary: my reasoning is an asset I don't want to lose to a context window.

nodes.title: The schema ships — four do_* tables, live
nodes.body: The first concrete code for 道: the four do_ tables and the do_verdict enum, live on the linked Supabase project, with a 12-assertion pgTAP RLS isolation test mirroring the field test.

nodes.title: The prototype, then a deliberate pause to read
nodes.body: Shipped the /rakusat/do/ batch-picker prototype, then re-scoped 道 into three stages (triage → data-driven review → strategy engine) and made canonical a three-tier data model: [F] Facts (a sync mirror, never updated), [O] Opinions (updated, each change auto-writing a diary event), [R] Results (append-only, amend-not-overwrite).

nodes.title: Mansion Manager
Conceived — keep records, proper, minimal energy
nodes.body: Scoped a second product: a management tool for owners like me — small or mid マンション / commercial buildings, no 管理会社, no resident manager.

nodes.title: Grounded on my real 17-room building
nodes.body: Anchored v1 in my actual building — 17 rooms, the agent handles rental and I do everything else: clean, check, empty the junk-mail cabin, minor repairs, turnovers, the occasional water-leak incident.

nodes.title: The lane: internal-first, but multi-tenant from day 0
nodes.body: Committed the lane honestly: "I am honest on 'facilitate myself'… even [if] nobody want[s it], I still need something.

nodes.title: Reuse the Rakusat diary engine
nodes.body: Decided to reuse the existing Rakusat diary module as the logbook rather than build fresh — and a recon confirmed it already fits: diary_monos (subject) + diary_events (timestamped: date, cost, notes, todo, media, follow-up) + per-team editable categories, already tenant-scoped with RLS.

nodes.title: Two pillars, and "all is about money"
nodes.body: Locked the spine around two pillars: (1) records → reports, and (2) a compliance schedule by Japanese law (消防設備点検, 貯水槽清掃, エレベーター検査, 12条点検…), where completing an obligation writes a record + expense + document that feeds the reports — closing the loop.

nodes.title: Market validation — the wedge holds
nodes.body: Research confirmed the hypothesis: Japan's small-owner segment has cheap rent tools with only a passive 修繕履歴 log, and forward 法定点検 schedulers that target 管理会社 pros at 20–30+ buildings — but nobody combines rent + a forward compliance schedule + a must/preference spend decision for small owners.

nodes.title: jjcashflow
Born, and cut loose
nodes.body: I started the Cashflow Diary as a genuinely separate product, and the same day gave the decisive order: make it standalone, sever every tie to jjconnect.online / rakusalab.

nodes.title: A two-sided "Diary Tree" — accountant side first
nodes.body: Pivoted the product to two sides.
refs: 会计事务所→客户→定期业务→提醒/沟通→单据

nodes.title: Stage A demo — live on jjconnect.tokyo
nodes.body: Shipped the accountant demo live: a mobile-first, all-fake-data, no-backend, no-auth walkthrough.

nodes.title: It's a wedge, not an accounting system
nodes.body: Captured the positioning into the plan: a cheap, mobile-first capture-and-workflow layer that sits in front of the accountant's existing software and feeds it — 不与 SaaS 竞争财税功能.

nodes.title: Real data — editable, savable, reloadable
nodes.body: Per my ask — "real editable, savable, reloadable" — the accountant workbench now persists to localStorage: in 我的数据 mode every change auto-saves, the landing offers "继续我的数据 (N 客户 · M 事件)," and there's full CRUD via a bottom-sheet modal.

nodes.title: The client side and real OCR — the 代为入力 wedge made tangible
nodes.body: Built the 客户记账侧 capture app and the receipt OCR that makes the wedge concrete: 📷 拍照记账 → client-side resize → AI parse → an editable draft with 発行者, 税込金额, per-rate 消費税 (10/8%), インボイス T+13 number, 勘定科目, and a confidence score → ✓ 记账.

nodes.title: OCR made provider-flexible — Gemini preferred
nodes.body: Made the parser pick its engine by env key: Gemini first (gemini-2.0-flash, cheapest, and I already use Gemini in rakusalab — so a separate key), else Claude, else mock.
